# HIMSHA Network

> An experimental Bitcoin programmability layer where every state transition is proven
> correct by a RISC Zero zero-knowledge receipt — not a validator majority vote. An
> educational proof of concept written in Rust. NOT audited; NOT for mainnet funds.

## What it is

- A Solana-style account + program model whose state transitions settle on Bitcoin L1.
- Programs execute deterministically; correctness is (or can be) backed by a ZK receipt
  rather than validator consensus.
- Built-in programs: system, token, ATA (associated token account), swap (constant-product
  AMM, x·y=k), lending (Bitcoin Ordinals collateral), money-market (interest-bearing
  cToken lending, supply/borrow/repay/liquidate), yield vault (ERC-4626-style, deploys
  idle assets into the money market via CPI), NFT metadata, runes, oracle.

## Core security model (enforced and covered by 182 passing tests)

- BIP-340 Schnorr transaction signature verification (secp256k1).
- Replay protection: signed `recent_blockhash` + `chain_id`, plus txid de-duplication.
- Account writability enforcement (only writable accounts can be mutated).
- Cross-program-invocation (CPI) depth limit.
- Atomic per-transaction state commits (all-or-nothing).
- ZK receipt binding gate: the node only persists a transition whose receipt commits to
  exactly the accounts produced (native integrity check).

## Under active development (contributions welcome)

- Cryptographic ZK proving via the `--features zkvm` RISC Zero path.
- Account owner enforcement, native compute metering, execution at block-inclusion time.
- Bitcoin L1 anchoring of block/state roots, consensus log replication + commit index,
  FROST/Taproot threshold custody for settlement, end-to-end Lightning verification.

## Technology

Rust · RISC Zero (zkVM) · Bitcoin (Taproot, Schnorr, BIP-340) · secp256k1 / FROST
threshold signatures · Lightning Network (LND) · redb (embedded storage) · Tokio (async)
· borsh (serialization) · JSON-RPC.
